news_summary: Recent discoveries reveal advanced ancient mathematical knowledge and highlight the importance of preserving the natural and cultural treasures of Gilgit-Baltistan amidst development and climate challenges.
news_summary_fr: Des découvertes récentes mettent en lumière des connaissances mathématiques anciennes très avancées et soulignent l'importance de préserver les trésors naturels et culturels du Gilgit-Baltistan face aux défis liés au développement et au changement climatique.
news_summary_es: Los recientes descubrimientos revelan un avanzado conocimiento matemático de la antigüedad y ponen de relieve la importancia de preservar los tesoros naturales y culturales de Gilgit-Baltistán en medio de los retos que plantean el desarrollo y el cambio climático.

articles.title: Mathematics formula found on Maya wall rivals insights of ancient masters
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02170-8Mathematical texts from a Maya site in Guatemala identify an astronomer called Sak Tahn Waax.
articles.content:
The Maya temple Tikal in Guatemala is about one day's walk from Xultun, where researchers discovered mathematical formula scribbled on the walls.Credit: Kryssia Campos/Getty

A mathematical formula inscribed on a wall at the Maya site of Xultun in Guatemala has revealed the name of an important Maya mathematician-astronomer for the first time. Researchers suggest Sak Tahn Waax, or ‘White-Chested Fox’, was a scholar comparable with mathematical giants of the past.

In a study published 14 July in the journal Antiquity1, Heather Hurst, an archaeologist at Skidmore College in Saratoga Springs, New York, and her colleagues describe their analysis of a mathematical text from a chamber in Xultun that was originally excavated in 20112.

The chamber’s walls are painted with human figures and hieroglyphic texts. These include mathematical calculations based on astronomical calendars, which were used by the Maya people to decide the timing of events such as the inaugurations of kings. Hurst and her colleagues suggest that the chamber was a workspace for scribes making codices in the mid-eighth century ad.

The authors analysed one set of hieroglyphs in particular, referred to as Text 19. Hurst says that this set of mathematical calculations expresses the relationships between several calendar systems in a playful manner that hasn’t been seen before in Mayan texts. “I think it was a mathematical flex. Somebody was saying ‘I’ve got this amazing pattern, and it’s so good it needs to be written down’. It was like, ‘Boom! Mic drop!’,” says Hurst.

“The discovery shows people that the Maya were very clever, creative, intellectually curious people who taught and learnt and sometimes did math for the sake of it,” says Eric Heller, an archaeologist at the University of Southern California Dornsife.

Identity revealed

Text 19 is a small, L-shaped group of eleven hieroglyphs with a combined height of about 10 centimetres. Hurst and her colleagues found that the first nine hieroglyphs of the set encode the Maya calendar and astronomical cycles.

The formula shows how one 2,920-day cycle could be divided up into the calendar units used by the Maya people. This 2,920-day cycle was important because it tied together key astronomical cycles, corresponding to both five Venus cycles (584 days each) and eight solar years (365 days each). However, the Text 19 calculations also relate the 2,920 days to Uinal (months with 20 days), Tzolkin (the 260-day sacred calendar), Tun (a year with 360 days) and Mars years of 780 days.

“It’s just super nerdy math,” says Hurst. The hieroglyphs also show only partial dates, which made them hard to decipher. “They’re doing this abbreviated shorthand, so they give you the first half of a notation and the second half is implied.”

The mathematical formula on Text 19 appear as glyphs.Credit: Photograph by G. Ware, courtesy of the San Bartolo-Xultun Regional Archaeological Project

Until now, the identities of the mathematician-astronomers behind such calculations had remained mysterious. Hurst and her colleagues found a phrase in the penultimate hieroglyph in Text 19 meaning “so says”. This was followed by the name Sak Tahn Waax, in the final hieroglyph, suggesting that the writer was taking or giving credit for the calculation. “We know it’s a male name because it’s missing a prefix,” she says.

The fact that the writer is named is significant, because it suggests that mathematicians were recognized in Maya society as much as artists were, says Gerardo Aldana, an anthropologist at the University of California, Santa Barbara.

Equalling the greats
articles.reasoning: The discovery of a mathematical formula and the identification of a Maya mathematician-astronomer with intellectual contributions comparable to ancient masters reveals significant cultural and scientific achievements of the Maya civilization. This finding broadens our understanding of historical knowledge systems and highlights the intellectual legacy of an ancient society, providing meaningful educational and cultural benefits to the general public.
articles.personality_title: Ancient Maya mathematician identified through wall formula in Guatemala
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Guatemala, the ancient Maya city of Xultun holds a chamber with walls covered in painted figures and hieroglyphs. These writings include complex mathematical calculations linked to Maya calendars and astronomy, used to plan important events.

**What happened** – Researchers studying the Xultun site discovered a mathematical formula on a wall that names a Maya scholar called Sak Tahn Waax, meaning ‘White-Chested Fox’. This formula, part of a group of hieroglyphs called Text 19, shows how the Maya connected different calendar cycles, like the 2,920-day cycle linked to Venus and the solar year. The discovery was published in July 2026 by archaeologist Heather Hurst and her team.

**Impact** – This finding is unique because it reveals the identity of a Maya mathematician for the first time and shows how advanced their math was. Sak Tahn Waax’s work compares to great mathematicians from other ancient cultures. It also proves that Maya mathematicians were respected in their society, much like artists.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will continue studying Maya texts to learn more about their math and astronomy. This could help us better understand how the Maya tracked time and planned events. It might also inspire new ways to teach math by showing how ancient people used it creatively.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A mathematical formula on a Maya wall has revealed the name of a skilled ancient mathematician, highlighting the Maya’s advanced knowledge and respect for intellectual work.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un mathématicien maya ancien identifié grâce à une formule murale au Guatemala
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au Guatemala, la cité maya ancienne de Xultun possède une chambre dont les murs sont couverts de figures peintes et de hiéroglyphes. Ces écritures comprennent des calculs mathématiques complexes liés aux calendriers et à l’astronomie maya, utilisés pour planifier des événements importants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs étudiant le site de Xultun ont découvert une formule mathématique sur un mur qui nomme un savant maya appelé Sak Tahn Waax, ce qui signifie « Renard à la poitrine blanche ». Cette formule, faisant partie d’un groupe de hiéroglyphes appelé Texte 19, montre comment les Mayas reliaient différents cycles calendaires, comme le cycle de 2 920 jours lié à Vénus et à l’année solaire. Cette découverte a été publiée en juillet 2026 par l’archéologue Heather Hurst et son équipe.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est unique car elle révèle pour la première fois l’identité d’un mathématicien maya et montre à quel point leurs mathématiques étaient avancées. Le travail de Sak Tahn Waax se compare à celui de grands mathématiciens d’autres civilisations anciennes. Elle prouve aussi que les mathématiciens mayas étaient respectés dans leur société, tout comme les artistes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier les textes mayas pour mieux comprendre leurs mathématiques et leur astronomie. Cela pourrait aider à mieux saisir comment les Mayas suivaient le temps et planifiaient leurs événements. Cela pourrait aussi inspirer de nouvelles façons d’enseigner les mathématiques en montrant comment les anciens les utilisaient de manière créative.

**Une phrase clé** – Une formule mathématique sur un mur maya a révélé le nom d’un mathématicien ancien talentueux, soulignant le savoir avancé des Mayas et le respect porté au travail intellectuel.
articles.personality_title_es: Matemático maya antiguo identificado gracias a una fórmula en una pared de Guatemala
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Guatemala, la antigua ciudad maya de Xultun tiene una cámara con paredes cubiertas de figuras pintadas y jeroglíficos. Estos escritos incluyen cálculos matemáticos complejos relacionados con calendarios y astronomía maya, usados para planear eventos importantes.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores que estudiaban el sitio de Xultun descubrieron una fórmula matemática en una pared que nombra a un sabio maya llamado Sak Tahn Waax, que significa ‘Zorro de pecho blanco’. Esta fórmula, parte de un grupo de jeroglíficos llamado Texto 19, muestra cómo los mayas conectaban diferentes ciclos del calendario, como el ciclo de 2,920 días relacionado con Venus y el año solar. El hallazgo se publicó en julio de 2026 por la arqueóloga Heather Hurst y su equipo.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es único porque revela por primera vez la identidad de un matemático maya y muestra lo avanzada que era su matemática. El trabajo de Sak Tahn Waax se compara con grandes matemáticos de otras culturas antiguas. También demuestra que los matemáticos mayas eran respetados en su sociedad, al igual que los artistas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando los textos mayas para aprender más sobre su matemática y astronomía. Esto podría ayudar a entender mejor cómo los mayas medían el tiempo y planificaban eventos. También podría inspirar nuevas formas de enseñar matemática mostrando cómo los antiguos la usaban de manera creativa.

**Una frase clave** – Una fórmula matemática en una pared maya reveló el nombre de un antiguo matemático habilidoso, destacando el avanzado conocimiento maya y el respeto por el trabajo intelectual.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an ancient Maya chamber wall adorned with intricate hieroglyphic glyphs and symbolic calendar cycles, illuminated softly by natural light filtering in, alongside a stylized white fox silhouette representing the mathematician Sak Tahn Waax, surrounded by abstract celestial motifs of Venus and the sun to evoke the advanced astronomical and mathematical knowledge of the Maya civilization.

articles.title: Gilgit-Baltistan: Pakistan's tourist jewel tested by development and climate change
articles.summary: In just a few decades, the Hunza Valley in Gilgit-Baltistan, a remote mountainous region of northern Pakistan, has undergone a dramatic transformation. Expanded access to education, improved healthcare and strong local development have reshaped the area, in a model of progress often linked to foundations associated with the Aga Khan, the spiritual leader of the Ismaili community. The minority counts nearly 500,000 members in Pakistan and makes up around 90% of Hunza's population.
articles.content:
One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site.

Reading time 1 min

In just a few decades, the Hunza Valley in Gilgit-Baltistan, a remote mountainous region of northern Pakistan, has undergone a dramatic transformation. Expanded access to education, improved healthcare and strong local development have reshaped the area, in a model of progress often linked to foundations associated with the Aga Khan, the spiritual leader of the Ismaili community. The minority counts nearly 500,000 members in Pakistan and makes up around 90% of Hunza's population.

Greater access to the region has also fuelled tourism, attracting tens of thousands of visitors each year to a landscape dominated by peaks above 7,000 metres, including K2. The influx has created new opportunities for local communities, but it is also changing the territory. Hotels and infrastructure are expanding rapidly, with concrete development becoming increasingly visible in some valleys.

At the same time, Gilgit-Baltistan is on the front line of climate change. The region's thousands of glaciers are retreating rapidly, while climate-related disasters are becoming more frequent, threatening an economy that is still finding its footing.

A report by Shahzaib Wahlah and Ondine de Gaulle.
articles.reasoning: The article describes significant positive development in the remote Hunza Valley, including expanded education, improved healthcare, and local development benefiting a large community. This progress model linked to the Aga Khan foundations has broad social impact. While it also highlights challenges from climate change and tourism, the core story focuses on meaningful, inspiring social advancement in a historically underserved region.
articles.personality_title: Hunza Valley’s growth shows positive change in remote Pakistan region
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Hunza Valley is a mountainous area in northern Pakistan, part of a region called Gilgit-Baltistan. For many years, it was difficult to reach and had limited services like schools and hospitals.

**What happened** – In recent decades, the Hunza Valley has seen big improvements. More schools and better healthcare have been built, mainly thanks to help from groups connected to Aga Khan, a leader of the Ismaili community living there. Nearly 90% of the people in Hunza belong to this community. Tourism has also increased, with many visitors coming to see the tall mountains like K2.

**Impact** – These changes have helped local people live healthier lives and get an education. The growing number of tourists brings money and jobs, but also means new buildings and roads are changing the natural landscape. At the same time, the region faces risks from climate change, as glaciers shrink and weather problems become more common.

**What’s next step** – The community and leaders will need to balance continuing development and tourism with protecting the environment. Finding ways to adapt to climate change will be important to keep the progress going.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Hunza Valley’s recent improvements in education, healthcare, and tourism show how careful development can bring positive change to a remote area, even while facing new challenges.
articles.personality_title_fr: La vallée de Hunza, exemple de progrès dans une région reculée du Pakistan
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La vallée de Hunza est une région montagneuse dans le nord du Pakistan, appelée Gilgit-Baltistan. Pendant longtemps, elle était difficile d’accès et manquait d’écoles et d’hôpitaux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Ces dernières décennies, la vallée de Hunza a beaucoup progressé. Plus d’écoles et de meilleurs soins de santé ont été construits, surtout grâce à l’aide de groupes liés à l’Aga Khan, leader de la communauté ismaélienne qui habite la région. Près de 90 % des habitants de Hunza appartiennent à cette communauté. Le tourisme a aussi augmenté, avec de nombreux visiteurs venus admirer les montagnes comme le K2.

**Impact** – Ces changements ont amélioré la vie des habitants, qui ont accès à la santé et à l’éducation. L’afflux de touristes apporte de l’argent et des emplois, mais aussi de nouvelles constructions qui modifient le paysage naturel. En même temps, la région est menacée par le changement climatique, avec la fonte des glaciers et des phénomènes météo plus fréquents.

**Prochaine étape** – La communauté et les dirigeants devront trouver un équilibre entre développement, tourisme et protection de l’environnement. S’adapter au changement climatique sera essentiel pour maintenir ces progrès.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les progrès récents en éducation, santé et tourisme dans la vallée de Hunza montrent comment un développement réfléchi peut transformer positivement une région isolée, malgré de nouveaux défis.
articles.personality_title_es: El valle de Hunza muestra avances positivos en una región remota de Pakistán
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El valle de Hunza es una zona montañosa en el norte de Pakistán, en una región llamada Gilgit-Baltistán. Durante mucho tiempo, fue difícil de acceder y tenía pocos servicios como escuelas y hospitales.

**Qué pasó** – En las últimas décadas, el valle de Hunza ha mejorado mucho. Se construyeron más escuelas y mejores servicios de salud, principalmente con ayuda de grupos vinculados al Aga Khan, líder de la comunidad ismailí que vive allí. Cerca del 90 % de la población de Hunza pertenece a esta comunidad. También ha crecido el turismo, con muchos visitantes que llegan para ver montañas altas como el K2.

**Impacto** – Estos cambios han ayudado a que la gente local tenga una vida más saludable y educación. El aumento del turismo trae dinero y empleos, pero también significa que nuevos edificios y caminos están cambiando el paisaje natural. Al mismo tiempo, la región enfrenta riesgos por el cambio climático, con glaciares que se derriten y más problemas meteorológicos.

**Próximo paso** – La comunidad y los líderes tendrán que equilibrar el desarrollo y el turismo con la protección del medio ambiente. Adaptarse al cambio climático será clave para continuar el progreso.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las mejoras recientes en educación, salud y turismo en el valle de Hunza muestran cómo un desarrollo cuidadoso puede traer cambios positivos a una zona remota, aunque enfrente nuevos desafíos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of the Hunza Valley’s majestic mountain peaks including a stylized representation of K2, with warm tones highlighting vibrant traditional village rooftops and blossoming apricot trees, alongside gently flowing rivers fed by melting glaciers depicted as softly glowing ice formations retreating into the distance, symbolizing both thriving local communities and the subtle impact of climate change.
